Brooklyn is already home to one of the better-organized urban mesh networks @nycmesh, including many parts of Brooklyn that have seen protests in recent days. Maybe this infrastructure can be reused. https://t.co/xs3xjM7Yob https://t.co/MxwehwztFM

Hong Kong also did some really interesting stuff using apps like Bridgefy, which works over Bluetooth mesh networks and does not depend on cell towers. https://t.co/niTERrCVT2 We should encourage folks to keep download these ahead of time and prepare for potential comms downages. https://t.co/7arwdBPYRj

A custody tracking app sounds really useful. Lots of privacy implications there to keep people safe, and would need to interface well with local jail support groups. Anyone have ideas on smart ways to accomplish this? https://t.co/7arwdBPYRj
RT @CapobiaNB: @mrejfox WiFi networks that can’t be jammed up ? Communication seems to be an issue. Tracking people in custody app? Just so…

In certain cities @OpenOversight has a public searchable database of law enforcement. Volunteers can sort photos and identify known officers. They also have a GitHub with 100+ open issues. What can we build tonight that can be used tomorrow? https://t.co/QHMVa9YRWZ
Hong Kong protesters famously had an app that let them track the locations of marches and police. What can we build tonight that can be used tomorrow? https://t.co/MtDDq21zRb
Here's a great tool by @everestpipkin to scrub metadata and blur faces in images, from a phone or computer. What can we build tonight that can be used tomorrow? https://t.co/w1dSA4dFb1
What apps/tools do protesters need that we can build quickly? What infrastructure can we prepare to help people organize safely? What can we build tonight that can be used tomorrow?
